title: Choosing a YOLO Version
concept: Selecting the right YOLO model requires balancing model accuracy, size, and real-time latency constraints of the target hardware.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main innovation of YOLOv10?

options.text: NMS-free object detection using dual label assignment

exercises.explain: Correct! YOLOv10 is designed to be NMS-free during deployment.

cards.title: The Developer's Choice
cards.body: With so many YOLO models, how does an engineer pick the right one? Do you always grab the newest version? Or is an older, mature model sometimes a safer bet? Let's decode the trade-offs.

cards.title: YOLO Version Selection
cards.body: Choosing a YOLO version involves balancing parameter count (model size), latency constraints (speed), deployment targets (CPU, GPU, or mobile TPU), and tooling support (stable APIs and export formats).

cards.title: Picking a Vehicle
cards.body: Picking a model size is like picking a vehicle. A tiny scooter (YOLOv8-Nano) is cheap, fast, and fits in small paths. A heavy truck (YOLOv8-XLarge) uses lots of gas (compute) but can carry massive loads (maximum accuracy).

cards.title: Mobile vs. Server
cards.body: A mobile shopping app uses YOLOv8-Nano (3M parameters) to run inside the phone's memory. A cloud-based medical scanner uses YOLOv9-e (50M parameters) to detect micro-defects with high precision, where latency is secondary.

cards.title: Newer is Always Better?
cards.body: You might think you must always use YOLOv10. Fact: YOLOv5 is still widely used in production because it is extremely stable, heavily tested on edge hardware, and has mature documentation and exports.

cards.title: YOLO Model Sizes
cards.body: Comparing scaling choices within a YOLO family:

left.text:
Parameter size: ~3M
Lowest latency, fits on edge CPU
Lower accuracy on small objects

right.text:
Parameter size: 40M+
Higher latency, requires server GPU
Maximum accuracy, picks up details

exercises.prompt: Which YOLO variant is best suited for deployment on a low-power edge device with strict memory limits?
exercises.explain: Correct! Nano/Tiny models are lightweight and designed for resource-constrained devices.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Older YOLO versions like YOLOv5 are sometimes chosen because their deployment pipelines are more mature and stable.
exercises.explain: True. Stability and compatibility with older hardware converters are common reasons to stick with mature releases.

exercises.sentence: As you scale from YOLO-Nano to YOLO-XLarge, the model accuracy generally increases but the inference speed ___.
exercises.explain: Larger models require more computations, which increases latency (decreases speed).

exercises.prompt: What is the key trade-off when selecting a YOLO model size?

options.text: Color accuracy vs. black and white speed

exercises.explain: Correct! You must trade off model accuracy (larger model) for inference speed (smaller model).

exercises.prompt: You are building a real-time face mask detector that runs on a security camera's built-in processor. It must process 30 frames per second. Which model choice makes the most sense?

options.text: A lightweight YOLOv8-Nano model, which easily achieves the 30 FPS target on edge hardware while maintaining acceptable accuracy.
A heavy YOLOv9-XLarge model running in the cloud, accepting video transmission delay.
A classification model with no bounding boxes.
Using a manually coded filter system instead of deep learning.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Real-time local processing is best handled by optimized tiny/nano model variants. Phase 8: Modern Object Detection is complete. Next up: Image Segmentation.
